nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving streams, rivers, and tributaries with sandy or leaf-litter substrates; often found near forest edges in soft, slightly acidic blackwater or clearwater conditions

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential for barbel protection)
driftwood and leaf litter for cover and tannins
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light

diet.notes: Sinking wafers, pellets, and tablets should form the dietary staple. Relishes frozen and live foods such as bloodworms, daphnia, tubifex, and brine shrimp. Will scavenge uneaten foods from the substrate but should not be relied upon solely as a scavenger. Feed after lights-out to align with natural crepuscular foraging behaviour.

goodWith: dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) at appropriate pH
avoidWith: fish requiring hard alkaline water incompatible with Corydoras parameters

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Corydoras melanistius; shows characteristic dark dorsum and spotted flanks.

summary: The Bluespotted Corydoras (Corydoras melanistius) is a small, peaceful catfish from Venezuela and Guyana, distinguished by a dark dorsum and iridescent blue-spotted body. Generally considered hardy and well suited to community tanks; best kept in groups of six or more over fine sand.
careNotes: The Bluespotted Corydoras is one of the more robust Corydoras species and makes an excellent choice for the beginner aquarist. Its most striking feature is the dark, almost black dorsum contrasting with a silvery-grey body patterned with iridescent bluish spots or speckles — especially vivid under good lighting. Like all Corydoras, it possesses hardened pectoral and dorsal fin spines that are mildly venomous and capable of causing a painful sting if handled carelessly; always use a soft mesh net and avoid pressing the fish against hard surfaces. Fine sand substrate (at least 3 cm deep) is essential — coarse gravel can abrade the delicate barbels and, once damaged, barbels are prone to bacterial infection. Maintain a group of at least 6; solitary individuals or small groups exhibit stress and reduced activity. Water should be soft to moderately hard and slightly acidic to neutral. The species is sensitive to poor water quality and elevated nitrates; weekly water changes of 25–30% are recommended. Avoid copper-based medications as with all callichthyids. Good filtration with a gentle flow and some driftwood or leaf litter to provide tannins and cover will keep this species in optimal condition. A 2024 taxonomic revision reassigns this species to the resurrected genus Hoplisoma, making the current valid name Hoplisoma melanistius, though Corydoras melanistius remains in widespread trade use.
breedingNotes: Breeding Corydoras melanistius in the aquarium is achievable with some preparation. Conditioning a group of 2–3 males per female on high-quality live or frozen foods precedes spawning. Spawning is often triggered by a partial water change with cooler, soft water simulating seasonal rainfall. Eggs are deposited on smooth surfaces — glass, broad plant leaves, or the underside of driftwood — and should be removed to a separate hatching container with gentle aeration to prevent fungus. Fry hatch in 3–5 days and can be fed infusoria, baby brine shrimp nauplii, and powdered fry foods.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras melanistius
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Orinoco and coastal drainages of Venezuela and Guyana), adult size (~4.5 cm SL), temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 2–15 dH, habitat description, diet, compatibility, and breeding notes. Also confirmed mildly venomous pectoral and dorsal spines and fine-sand substrate requirement.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras melanistius
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), adult size (up to 5.5 cm TL), native range (Venezuela, Guyana), temperature range, and diet. Confirms species is benthic omnivore. Notes synonym Hoplisoma melanistius from 2024 Dias & Britto revision.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: General Corydoras care guide; confirmed fine sand substrate requirement, group size minimum of 6, sinking food preference, and beginner-level care rating. Venomous spines handling caution noted for the genus.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras species profiles
additional.notes: Supplementary source for Corydoras genus husbandry; confirmed soft-water preference, crepuscular feeding behaviour, barbel sensitivity to coarse substrate, and mildly venomous spine caution for the Callichthyidae family.

additional.name: Dias & Britto (2024) — Revalidation of Hoplisoma (Siluriformes: Callichthyidae)
additional.notes: Taxonomic reference for 2024 Dias & Britto reclassification of Corydoras melanistius to Hoplisoma melanistius. New name is pending widespread trade adoption; Corydoras melanistius retained as primary scientific name in this entry per current trade usage.

fish.breedingNotes: Conditioning a group with live or frozen foods, then performing a partial water change with cooler, softer water, will generally trigger spawning. Eggs are deposited on glass, broad plant leaves, or other smooth surfaces; move them promptly to a separate container with gentle aeration to prevent fungal growth. Fry typically begin accepting baby brine shrimp nauplii and powdered fry food from around day three or four.
